China Pacific Insurance Co., Ltd. Reports Earnings Results for the Full Year Ended December 31, 2025
data.description:
China Pacific Insurance (Group) Co., Ltd. reported earnings results for the full year ended December 31, 2025. For the full year, the company reported net income was CNY 53,505 million compared to CNY 44,960 million a year ago. Basic earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 5.56 compared to CNY 4.67 a year ago.

Diluted earnings per share from continuing operations was CNY 5.54 compared to CNY 4.67 a year ago.

Judge dismisses lawsuit by Musk's X Corp accusing advertisers of illegal boycott
data.description:
WASHINGTON, March 26 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge on Thursday dismissed X Corp's antitrust lawsuit that accused the World Federation of Advertisers and a group of major companies including Mars, CVS Health and Colgate-Palmolive of illegally boycotting billionaire entrepreneur Elon Musk's social media company.

U.S. District Judge Jane Boyle in the federal court in Dallas said Musk's X failed to show that it had suffered any harm under federal antitrust laws.

(Reporting by Mike Scarcella; Editing by David Bario)

Meta and Google Found Liable; Corebridge and Equitable Merge; Pony AI Swings to Profit
data.description:
Today's biggest winners and losers in the stock market.

On this episode of Stock Movers:

- Corebridge Financial (CRBG) and Equitable Holdings (EQH) to combine in an all-stock merger, valuing the combined company at approximately $22 billion.

- Meta Platforms Inc. (META) and Alphabet Inc.'s Google (GOOG, GOOGL) must pay damages to a 20-year-old woman who said her addiction to social media caused her mental health struggles, a jury concluded in a landmark decision that could signal hefty risks for the companies as they fight thousands of similar claims.

- Pony AI Inc. (PONY) delivered its first profitable quarter ever, bolstered by a windfall from an early investment, rather than its main robotaxi business.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: Novo Nordisk appoints Mars CEO as board observer
data.description:
COPENHAGEN, March 26 (Reuters) - Novo Nordisk on Thursday elected Poul Weihrauch, CEO of family-owned U.S. candy and pet food giant Mars, as observer to its board, as the drugmaker seeks to strengthen its position in the highly competitive U.S. obesity market.

The maker of weight-loss drug Wegovy announced the appointment at its annual general meeting, where shareholders also elected pharmaceutical industry veterans Jan van de Winkel and Ramona Sequeira to the board, along with Helena Saxon, a board member at fashion retailer H&M.

In 2025, Novo Nordisk and its majority shareholder, the Novo Nordisk Foundation, carried out a leadership shake-up, replacing the CEO and restructuring the board.

This included appointing Lars Rebien Sorensen, who also chairs the Foundation, as board chairman, consolidating his leadership role.

Novo is trying to boost its consumer credentials in the U.S. market in a number of ways under its new management. In January it launched its Wegovy pill across multiple cash-pay channels, rather than solely through traditional insurance routes.

The company is leaning into telehealth, retail partnerships and direct-to-consumer access as it and chief rival Eli Lilly and Co say the obesity market is becoming increasingly "consumer-like".

(Reporting by Stine Jacobsen and Maggie Fick, editing by Anna Ringstrom and Barbara Lewis)

data.title: BAE Systems, Scale AI Form Partnership Focused on Agentic AI in Defense
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller


BAE Systems has formed a partnership with Scale AI to accelerate the development of AI capabilities for military operations.

The partnership brings BAE's experience in defense operations and systems integration together with Scale's suite of AI technology.

The companies intend to integrate Scale's agentic AI capabilities into the architecture of U.S. military combat vehicles and deterrence programs.


Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-26-26 1109ET

Apple Bolsters US Manufacturing with New Partnerships
data.description:
Apple has announced the addition of 4 new partners—Bosch, Cirrus Logic, TDK, and Qnity Electronics—to its US supply chain. The group plans to invest $400m by 2030 to support the domestic production of strategic components under its American Manufacturing Program. This initiative is part of a broader $600bn 4-year commitment aimed at strengthening innovation and industrial capacity within the United States.

The new partners will play a pivotal role in manufacturing essential components. TDK will produce iPhone camera sensors, Bosch will manufacture integrated circuits at a TSMC facility in Washington State, and Cirrus Logic will develop semiconductors with GlobalFoundries in New York. Qnity Electronics is set to provide materials and technologies related to semiconductors and high-performance computing. Apple also continues to collaborate with major players such as TSMC in Arizona and GlobalFoundries.

Since the program's inception, Apple has already exceeded its initial targets, with over 20 billion chips produced across 24 plants in 12 states. Notably, the group expects to procure more than 100 million advanced chips from TSMC's Arizona site in 2026. This strategy is designed to enhance supply chain resilience and reduce foreign dependency, as the company has already absorbed approximately $3.3bn in tariff-related costs.

Trump says Venezuela's Maduro to face additional charges
data.description:
WASHINGTON, March 26 (Reuters) - The U.S. will bring additional cases against deposed Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, President Donald Trump told reporters during a cabinet meeting at the White House on Thursday.

Maduro, who was captured by U.S. forces during a raid in Venezuela in January, currently faces narcoterrorism and narcotics-related charges in New York.

(Reporting by Steve Holland and Gram Slattery; editing by Scott Malone)

AI is Driving a Real-Time Recalibration Says Berstein
data.description:
Brad Bernstein, Managing Partner at FTV Capital, joins Paul Sweeney and Tom Keene to discuss how AI exuberance is driving both dislocation and opportunity. Bernstein says with AI, we're in the early innings of a major platform shift similar to cloud or mobile, but with much more uncertainty around how it will play out.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: In South Korea, a plastics maker feels the force of war far away
data.description:
STORY: It's a long way from the Middle East.... but this South Korean plastic film factory suddenly finds itself at the mercy of global politics. 

The facility in the city of Ansan has slashed production amid the war in Iran.

Factory manager Choi Gun-soo said the conflict has triggered shortages in raw materials.

"We usually have the capacity to produce 100 tons per day. But right now, we're producing at most 20-30 tons. That means it's only 20-30% of our usual output. As I mentioned earlier, since we're out of raw materials for some products, we'll have to gradually shut down the machines, and the next one to two weeks is likely to be very critical."

:: CENTCOM

The conflict in the Middle East has brought severe disruption to global energy markets.

Tehran has retaliated against U.S.-Israeli attacks by closing the Strait of Hormuz, through which around a fifth of the world's oil is shipped. 

That's a big problem in South Korea, which gets about 70% of its crude oil via the Strait. 

Choi said the factory mainly sourced its raw materials from the Middle East.

"We've been through the oil shock and COVID-19, we've been running this company for 57 years. We've steadily focused on a single field, but this is the first time we've been hit this hard. We were really flustered, and we're in an urgent situation where we don't know when the machines might shut down."

At supermarkets in Seoul, shoppers are worried about resulting shortages and price hikes.

This student was among those stocking up: 

"I was worried that garbage bags would get more expensive, so I bought ten 20-liter ones. I also bought lots of packs of ramen yesterday, because the cost of plastic packaging probably accounts for a big part of the product's price."

Earlier this week, South Korean President Lee Jae Myung called for a nationwide energy-saving campaign, saying that public institutions would cut back on their use of passenger cars.

The government will also ask the top 50 oil-consuming firms to cut their use.
